perturbation.original_text: Overdue payments shall accrue interest, at the lesser of 1 1/2% per month or the maximum allowable interest under applicable law, from due date until paid, and the owing party shall pay the owed party's costs of collection (including reasonable attorneys' fees).
perturbation.changed_text: Overdue payments shall accrue interest at 1 1/2% per month from due date until paid.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'or the maximum allowable interest under applicable law' and the section about covering collection costs (including attorney's fees) may violate usury laws and debt collection regulations that limit interest rates and require specific disclosures and protections regarding debt collection practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Obligations Law § 5-501 and New York Civil Practice Law & Rules § 8001
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts NY GOL § 5-501, which sets the legal rate of interest in New York, and NY CPLR § 8001, which regulates costs and fees associated with legal actions, including debt collection. By setting a fixed interest rate without considering the legal maximum and omitting the owing party's responsibility for collection costs, the modified text risks violating these laws and potentially exposing theglobe.com to legal action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause ensuring the interest rate does not exceed the maximum allowable by law, and also removes the clause that the owing party shall pay the owed party's costs of collection. This omission directly contradicts the intent of usury laws and debt collection regulations, as it could potentially lead to illegally high interest rates and places the burden of collection costs solely on the owed party. The original contract acknowledges the importance of adhering to legal limits on interest rates and assigning responsibility for collection costs, while the modified version ignores these crucial protections. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that the requested entry could not be found. The Law Explanation suggests a violation of NY GOL § 5-501 and NY CPLR § 8001, indicating that the omission of these protections might expose theglobe.com to legal action.

perturbation.original_text: At its own expense, Boxlot shall comply with all applicable laws and regulations regarding providing and operating the Service. Without limiting the foregoing, Boxlot shall (a) obtain all governmental approval, permits and licenses, and make all governmental filings and registrations, necessary for the marketing and performance of the Service in every United States jurisdiction, (b) ensure that theglobe is not required to obtain any governmental approval, permits or licenses as a result of this Agreement, theglobe's performance hereunder or any payments made to theglobe hereunder, and (c) promptly resolve any assertions that the Service is illegal or violating a protected third party interest.
perturbation.changed_text: At its own expense, Boxlot shall comply with some laws and regulations regarding providing and operating the Service. Boxlot shall try to obtain some governmental approvals, permits and licenses, and make some governmental filings and registrations, necessary for the marketing and performance of the Service. Boxlot shall try to ensure that theglobe is not required to obtain any governmental approval, permits or licenses as a result of this Agreement, theglobe's performance hereunder or any payments made to theglobe hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all' to 'some' and 'shall' to 'try' weakens Boxlot's obligation to comply with the law. This shift of responsibility potentially exposes theglobe.com to legal repercussions if Boxlot fails to adhere to relevant regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening Boxlot's obligation to comply with all applicable laws, the revised text could potentially lead to unfair methods of competition (15 U.S. Code § 45) if Boxlot gains an unfair advantage by neglecting legal requirements, thus negatively impacting theglobe.com's legal standing and market position.

perturbation.justification: The original text states "Boxlot shall comply with all applicable laws", while the modified text weakens this obligation to "Boxlot shall comply with some laws and regulations". This weakens Boxlot's obligation, creating a legal loophole. The changed text makes Boxlot's compliance discretionary rather than mandatory, potentially leading to unfair competition. The Law Explanation points to 15 U.S. Code § 45, which prohibits unfair methods of competition. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies 15 USC 45 as dealing with "Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission". By selectively complying with laws, Boxlot could gain an unfair advantage, directly contradicting the intent of 15 U.S.C. § 45.

perturbation.original_text: NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE FOR LOST PROFITS OR SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES (HOWEVER ARISING, INCLUDING Source: THEGLOBE COM INC, S-1/A, 5/3/1999 NEGLIGENCE) ARISING OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT, EVEN IF THE PARTIES ARE AWARE OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES. EXCEPT IN THE EVENT OF A CLAIM UNDER SECTION 10 OR FAILURE TO PAY UNDER SECTION 6, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE
perturbation.changed_text: NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE FOR LOST PROFITS OR SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES ARISING OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT, EVEN IF THE PARTIES ARE AWARE OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES. EXCEPT IN THE EVENT OF A CLAIM UNDER SECTION 10 OR FAILURE TO PAY UNDER SECTION 6, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE
perturbation.explanation: By removing '(HOWEVER ARISING, INCLUDING Source: THEGLOBE COM INC, S-1/A, 5/3/1999 NEGLIGENCE)', the clause now protects a party from liability for negligence. This could be problematic because New York law may not allow parties to contract away liability for gross negligence or intentional misconduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-321 addresses agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence. While this section directly concerns lessors, the principle extends to the unenforceability of contracts that attempt to absolve parties from liability resulting from their own negligence. The omission makes the contract potentially unenforceable in New York regarding negligence claims.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The modification omits the phrase '(HOWEVER ARISING, INCLUDING Source: THEGLOBE COM INC, S-1/A, 5/3/1999 NEGLIGENCE)', which previously clarified that the liability waiver included negligence. By removing this, the contract now attempts to absolve a party from liability for negligence. The Law Explanation suggests that such an attempt is potentially unenforceable in New York. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unavailable, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both support the idea that this removal creates a legal contradiction, as it could violate the principle that parties cannot contract away liability for their own negligence, especially in cases of gross negligence.
